exemplar.title: Modelling the relationship between the length and natural frequency of a ruler

detailedExemplarData.title: Modelling the relationship between the length and natural frequency of a ruler

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Tables and graphs are wellâlabelled, scaled and include regression equations with RÂ² values
Data processing (frequency calculation, powerâlaw regression and linearisation) is accurate and methodologically sound
Communication of both raw and transformed data is generally clear and precise
weaknesses: Uncertainty propagation is incomplete and error bars are omitted without sufficient justification
No combined or percentage uncertainties are carried forward into the calibration constant
Residual analysis and a more rigorous uncertainty propagation approach are absent
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data processing is carried out appropriately and accurately, and communication is clear. However, significant omissions in uncertainty treatment and analysis of systematic errors prevent full alignment with the 5â6 band, placing this criterion in the 3â4 band at a score of 4.

strengths: Research question anchored in a specific, wellâexplained context linked to cantileverâbeam theory
Comprehensive methodology with stepâbyâstep procedures, diagrams, equipment list and clear sampling parameters
Independent, dependent and control variables are clearly identified and justified, supporting reproducibility
weaknesses: Depth of realâworld applications is limited (e.g., musical instruments, sensor design)
Theoretical assumptions (small deflections, uniform beam) are not explicitly discussed
Minor ambiguities remain in peakâpicking protocol and rationale for length increments
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student situates the research question within a specific and appropriate context and provides a reproducible method that clearly exceeds the 3â4 band. Some theoretical and practical details are missing, placing the work at the lower end of the 5â6 band and warranting a score of 5.

strengths: Conclusion restates the inverseâsquare relationship and acknowledges empirical deviations
Some comparison to scientific context via cantileverâbeam theory and damping effects
weaknesses: Overstates support for the hypothesis despite the exponent deviation of 1.66
No statistical test (e.g., t-test) to assess the significance of the deviation
Lacks quantitative comparison to published scientific data (e.g., EI values, damping ratios)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is described and partially consistent with the analysis, demonstrating some relevant comparison to accepted scientific context but lacking full justification. This aligns with the lower half of the 3â4 band, meriting a score of 3.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (sampling rate, amplitude decay, displacement control) are identified
Realistic improvements are proposed (e.g., higher sampling rate)
weaknesses: Suggested improvements lack detail on implementation and expected impact
Alternative enhancements (automated peak detection, different sensors) are omitted
Impact of identified weaknesses is described qualitatively without quantification
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student states realistic improvements and describes specific methodological limitations, but the explanations are brief and lack quantification. This performance fits the top of the 1â2 band, justifying a score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student situates the research question within a very specific context â vibration of a cantilevered ruler â and links it explicitly to the well-known cantilever-beam equation, clearly defining why length alone will be varied.  The brief discussion of how other beam parameters are absorbed into a constant shows the student understands the physical significance of the variables involved.  Because the background, theory and everyday observation are tightly connected to the framed question, the context meets the descriptor for the 5-6 band, though some depth on real-world applications (e.g. musical instruments or sensors) is missing, so the strand is awarded the lower end of that band (5).

Uncertainties in length (Â±0.5 mm) and time (Â±0.001 s) are identified and propagated to frequency (Â±0.002 Hz), yet the decision to omit error bars because they are âtoo small to graphâ reflects limited consideration of how they influence gradient or intercept.  No combined or percent uncertainty is carried forward to the constant k, nor is uncertainty in peak selection or damping discussed.  These omissions match the descriptor of âsome significant omissionsâ, warranting a mid-band (3) award.

The student recognises the need for multiple trials, a wide length range and small length intervals for higher precision rulers.  Relevant controls (initial displacement, mass stabilisation, coil alignment) are stated and mostly justified.  While these considerations are described, the explanation of why three trials or why ten periods were chosen, and an evaluation of sampling-rate adequacy before data collection, are missing.  Thus the work satisfies âdescribedâ rather than fully âexplained,â placing it solidly in the upper half of the 3-4 band.

Specific weaknessesâsampling-rate limitation, decreasing oscillation amplitude, difficulty maintaining constant displacementâare described, and their plausible influence on accuracy is outlined.  The explanation of relative impact (e.g., effect on small-length data) is mentioned but not quantified, matching the âdescribesâ rather than âexplainsâ level of the 3-4 band; hence a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

Step-by-step instructions, diagrams, photographs, a full equipment list, and explicit settings for the data-logger (1 kHz sampling, ten oscillations, repeat three times) enable straightforward reproduction.  Controlled variables and alignment procedures are described with sufficient detail to replicate clamp positions and magnet/coil spacing.  Minor ambiguity remains in how the peak-picking was carried out and in the exact range increments for the wooden ruler, so the work does not quite merit the very top of the band, but it clearly exceeds the 3-4 requirements.

Frequency is correctly derived from 10-period timing, and a power-law regression followed by an appropriate linearisation demonstrates sound processing.  Calculations are accurate, and the RÂ² analysis is relevant.  Minor issues (rounding and a single example calculation only) prevent a perfect 6, but the overall quality clearly fulfils the âappropriately and accuratelyâ descriptor.

The student proposes few improvements beyond recognising the need for a higher sampling rate or acknowledging displacement difficulties; these are realistic but stated briefly without detail on implementation or expected effect.  No suggestion addresses data-analysis shortcomings or explores alternative sensors.  This restricted treatment satisfies the basic ârealistic improvements are statedâ description, awarding 2 at the top of the 1-2 band.

Tables include units and uncertainties; graphs are appropriately scaled, labelled and accompanied by regression equations and RÂ² values.  Both the raw (frequency vs length) and transformed (frequency vs 1/LÂ²) presentations demonstrate clear processing.  A full raw-data appendix is not shown, so some precision evidence is inferred, but overall communication is simultaneously clear and precise, justifying a score of 5 within the top band.

Reference is made to cantilever-beam theory and damping effects in real materials, providing some relevant comparison to accepted scientific context.  However, the discussion remains qualitative and cites only two sources without deeper quantitative linkage (e.g., using published EI values).  This fits the descriptor of âsome relevant comparisonâ rather than a fully justified one, meriting 3.

The conclusion restates the inverse-square hypothesis but admits an empirical exponent of 1.66 and a non-zero intercept.  This partial alignment shows the student is aware of discrepancies, yet the judgement that the hypothesis is âvery likely correctâ is not fully consistent with the quantitative deviation.  Support is therefore incomplete, aligning with the 3-4 band at the lower score of 3.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated but could be refined to include the expected form of the relationship, specifying the exponent to guide experimental design.
Independent and dependent variables are clearly described with units and control methodology, supporting repeatability of the experiment.
The controlled variables table omits environmental factors (temperature, humidity) that can affect material stiffness; consider adding these controls or comment on their negligible effect.
The introduction vividly describes the initial idea but would benefit from linking to broader real-world applications (e.g., instrument design or vibration sensors) to strengthen contextual framing.

The derivation of $$f=\frac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\frac{1}{L^2}\sqrt{\tfrac{EI}{\rho A}}$$ is presented without discussing assumptions (e.g., small deflections, uniform beam), which should be stated for clarity.
Background theory outlines beam vibrations but would be strengthened by a citation for the formula source or a brief derivation to demonstrate understanding of its origin.
Discussion of nonzero intercept notes systematic error but no quantitative estimate of damping effect is given; calculate damping constant or add a model.
Conclusion overstates support for the hypothesis despite the empirical exponent of 1.66; apply a statistical test (e.g., tâtest) to assess deviation from theory.
The scientific context cites tuning forks but lacks quantitative comparison (e.g., published EI values or damping ratios); include such data for rigorous justification.
Improvement suggestions omit enhancements to data analysis (e.g., automated peak detection) or alternative sensors; propose realistic solutions like highâspeed camera or photogate.
Bibliography cites credible sources but omits references on ruler material properties; include a materials characterization paper to support discussion.
Figure lacks a caption and scale bar, making it difficult to interpret dimensions or compare to the textual description; please add both.
Quantification of how weights reduced vibrations is not provided; consider measuring and stating residual amplitude to justify retortâstand stabilization.
The hypothesis is restated but lacks mention of expected exponent value from theory; specifying the predicted exponent (2) would sharpen the experimental aim.
Material list is comprehensive but the quantity column for âRetort stand claw clampâ heading is blank; ensure table completeness for reproducibility.
Method section describes discarded approaches qualitatively; adding quantitative reasons (e.g., signalâtoânoise ratio, frame rate limitations) would clarify decision making.
Sampling rate set to 1 kHz is noted but no discussion of Nyquist criterion or potential aliasing; include justification of sampling adequacy.
Procedure step clearly details coil positioning and displacement control, enhancing reproducibility of measurements.
The electromagnetic induction explanation lacks mention of field strength (B) and coil geometry calibration; include these to support proportionality assumptions.
Vibrationâdamping measures are described qualitatively; suggest quantifying residual vibration amplitude postâmass loading to validate effectiveness.
Procedure stops when recording ends but omits mention of data handling steps (e.g., file naming, storage); include these for full reproducibility.
Data collection section lacks a summary of sample sizes per length and ruler type; add a concise table or statement to clarify experimental design.
Manual selection of peaks introduces potential bias and error; consider automated peakâpicking algorithms or repeatability tests.
Step size change for the plastic ruler is noted but rationale for different resolution (2.5 cm vs. 5 cm) is not provided; clarify decision.
Raw data table presents individual trials clearly but omits averages and standard deviations, which are necessary to assess variability.
Figure screenshot is not numbered consistently with earlier figures and lacks full caption; use unified numbering and descriptive caption.
Headers indicate measurement uncertainties for length and time but no demonstration of how these propagate to frequency in the raw data; add a step showing uncertainty calculation.
The example calculation could be expanded with algebraic steps before inversion to improve clarity for readers unfamiliar with the method.
The uncertainty in frequency is derived as Â±0.002 Hz by doubling time error, which is incorrect; apply proper uncertainty propagation (ÎfâÎT/TÂ²).
Axis labels and scales for the âLength vs Frequencyâ graph are not shown in the description; include axis ranges and units to ensure clarity.
Power-law regression exponent is discussed but not compared numerically to the theoretical exponent of 2, missing a key evaluation of model accuracy.
When presenting the reciprocal plot, regression slope and intercept uncertainties are omitted; include error estimates to assess confidence in linear fit.
Claiming 99% explanation of variance is valid, but a residual plot analysis of deviations is needed to identify systematic error patterns.
Discussion of methodological weaknesses is descriptive but does not quantify relative impact; consider estimating how decreased amplitude or sampling limits bias specific data points.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title:
How does increasing the length of a rubber band affect
its fundamental frequency?

userSubmission.researchQuestion:
How does increasing the length of a rubber band affect
its fundamental frequency?

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.

userSubmission.researchQuestion:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
